brief.topic: a kid helps paint part of their classroom mural and discovers that the right brush makes a big difference
brief.setting: a Texas elementary school art class

passageRow.title: Jada's Mural Adventure
passageRow.body:
## Jada's Mural Adventure

Jada was excited. Her art class would paint a mural. The mural would be in the school hallway. Jada loved to paint.

Mrs. Lopez, the art teacher, gave everyone a brush. Jada got a big brush. Some friends got small brushes. They all picked bright colors.

Jada wanted to paint a big sun. She dipped her brush into yellow paint. She tried to make a circle. But the brush was too big. The sun looked messy.

Jada felt a little upset. She wanted her sun to be perfect. Mrs. Lopez noticed and came over.

"Try this brush," Mrs. Lopez said. She handed Jada a smaller brush. It looked just right.

Jada took the new brush. She dipped it into the yellow paint again. This time, she could make the sun round. It was neat and bright.

"Wow, Jada, your sun looks great!" said her friend, Mia.

Jada smiled. She felt proud. The right brush made a big difference. She finished her sun and added some clouds.

When the mural was done, everyone admired it. Jada's sun shone brightly in the hallway. Jada learned that sometimes a small change can help a lot.

Jada loved the mural. She was glad she helped make it. Her art class had created something beautiful together.

brief.topic: a kid sleeps in a tent for the first time on a family camping trip in the Big Thicket and listens to the night sounds
brief.setting: Big Thicket National Preserve, East Texas

passageRow.title: First Night in the Big Thicket
passageRow.body:
## First Night in the Big Thicket

Hiro zipped up the tent door. It was his first time sleeping in a tent. His family was camping in the Big Thicket. The trees were tall and thick. The air smelled fresh.

Hiro lay down on his sleeping bag. He listened to the sounds outside. He heard the wind in the trees. The leaves rustled softly.

"Do you hear the crickets?" asked his dad. Hiro nodded. The crickets were loud and busy. Hiro had never heard so many before.

A frog croaked nearby. Hiro smiled. He liked the sound. It was like a funny song.

As the night got darker, more sounds joined in. An owl hooted from a tree. Hiro felt a little scared. His dad said, "It's just an owl. It won't bother us."

Hiro watched shadows dance on the tent walls. The moonlight made them move. It was like a show just for him.

Hiro's mom whispered, "Look up, Hiro." Through the tent's top, he saw stars. They twinkled like tiny lights.

Hiro felt cozy and safe. He closed his eyes. The night sounds were like a lullaby. Soon, he was asleep, dreaming of the forest.

In the morning, Hiro woke up to birds singing. He stretched and smiled. Sleeping in a tent was fun. He couldn't wait to explore more of the Big Thicket with his family.

brief.topic: a kid visits cousins on a small Hill Country farm and learns to scatter chicken feed evenly so all the chickens get some
brief.setting: a small farm in the Hill Country

passageRow.title: Feeding the Flock
passageRow.body:
## Feeding the Flock

Levi was excited to visit his cousins on their small farm. The sun was just rising over the Hill Country, and the air was fresh.

His cousins, Emma and Jack, welcomed him warmly. "Do you want to help us feed the chickens?" Emma asked.

Levi nodded eagerly. He had never fed chickens before. It sounded fun.

Emma handed Levi a bucket full of chicken feed. "You need to scatter it evenly," she explained. "Otherwise, some chickens won't get enough to eat."

Levi followed Emma to the chicken coop. The chickens clucked and pecked at the ground, waiting for their breakfast.

Jack showed Levi how to toss the feed gently. "Like this," he said, spreading the grains around.

Levi tried to copy Jack. At first, he threw too much in one spot. Some chickens got most of it, while others missed out.

"Try again," Jack encouraged. "Spread it out more."

Levi took a deep breath and tried again. This time, he tossed the feed more evenly. The chickens seemed happier, pecking all around.

"Great job!" Emma cheered. "Now everyone can eat."

Levi smiled, feeling proud. He had learned something new today. Feeding chickens was more fun than he had thought.

Afterward, they all sat on the porch, sipping lemonade. Levi watched the chickens roam around, content and full.

"Thanks for helping," Emma said. "Come back anytime."

Levi nodded. He couldn't wait to visit again. The farm was full of surprises and new things to learn.

brief.topic: a kid in second grade makes a poster for a school water-conservation contest and learns three ways to save water at home

passageRow.title: Aaliyah's Water-Wise Poster
passageRow.body:
## Aaliyah's Water-Wise Poster

Aaliyah was excited. Her teacher, Mrs. Brown, announced a water-conservation contest. Aaliyah loved drawing and wanted to make the best poster.

After school, Aaliyah sat at her kitchen table. She thought about ways to save water. Her mom, who was washing dishes, smiled at her. "You can start by turning off the tap while brushing your teeth," she said.

Aaliyah wrote it down: "Turn off the tap while brushing."

Next, Aaliyah asked her dad for advice. He was watering the garden. "We can use a rain barrel," he said. "It catches rainwater for plants."

Aaliyah added this idea to her poster too: "Use a rain barrel."

Finally, Aaliyah remembered something she learned in class. Long showers waste water. "Take shorter showers," she wrote in big letters.

The next day, Aaliyah brought her poster to school. She showed it to Mrs. Brown.

"Great work, Aaliyah!" Mrs. Brown said. "These are all excellent ways to save water."

Aaliyah smiled. She felt proud. Even if she didn't win, she knew she was helping the planet.

At the end of the week, the winner was announced. Aaliyah's poster didn't win first place, but it got a special mention.

Aaliyah learned that saving water was important. She promised to keep doing her part at home.

brief.topic: a kid practices for the second-grade spelling bee with a parent and learns a trick for remembering hard words

passageRow.title: Spelling Bee Practice
passageRow.body:
## Spelling Bee Practice

Camila sat at the kitchen table. Her mom was across from her. The table was covered with papers. Camila was practicing for the second-grade spelling bee.

"Let's try a hard word," her mom said. "How about 'butterfly'?"

Camila thought for a moment. "B-u-t-t-e-r-f-l-y," she spelled out loud.

"That's right!" her mom cheered. "Now try 'beautiful'."

Camila frowned. "That's a tricky one," she said. She tried to remember the letters. Her mom smiled and said, "I have a trick for you."

"What's the trick?" Camila asked.

Her mom wrote the word on a piece of paper. She underlined the first part. "Think of it like this: 'b-e-a' like in 'bee'. Then 'u-t-i-f-u-l' like 'you-tiful.' Can you say it?"

Camila nodded. "Bee-you-tiful," she repeated.

"Great!" her mom said. "Now try to spell it."

Camila closed her eyes. She imagined the word in her mind. Then she said, "B-e-a-u-t-i-f-u-l. Beautiful!"

"You got it!" her mom said happily. Camila felt proud. She knew she could remember it now.

They practiced more words. Each time Camila learned a new trick, she smiled. She felt ready for the spelling bee.

"Thanks, Mom," Camila said. "I think I'm ready."

Her mom gave her a hug. "You'll do great, Camila," she said. "Just remember to use your tricks."

Camila nodded. She was excited to show what she had learned.

brief.topic: three Texas rivers a second-grader should know — the Rio Grande (border), the Brazos (longest in Texas), and the Colorado (runs through Austin)

passageRow.title: Rivers of Texas
passageRow.body:
## Rivers of Texas

Rivers are important to Texas. They help with water, farming, and fun. Let's learn about three major rivers in Texas.

The **Rio Grande** is a famous river. It forms the border between Texas and Mexico. The Rio Grande is very long. It starts in Colorado and flows all the way to the Gulf of Mexico. People use its water for farming and drinking.

Next is the **Brazos River**. This is the longest river in Texas. The Brazos flows through many cities like Waco and College Station. It is important for water and recreation. People like to fish and boat on the Brazos.

Another important river is the **Colorado River**. It runs through Austin, the capital of Texas. This river is not as long as the Rio Grande or Brazos, but it is still important. The Colorado River provides water for people and power for the city.

Each river has its own role in Texas. The Rio Grande helps form a border. The Brazos is the longest and flows through many places. The Colorado River supplies water to Austin. These rivers help make Texas a special place.

brief.topic: the Guadalupe bass is the Texas state fish; it lives in clear Hill Country streams; it is found nowhere else in the world

passageRow.title: The Guadalupe Bass: A Texas Treasure
passageRow.body:
## The Guadalupe Bass: A Texas Treasure

The Guadalupe bass is a special fish. It is the state fish of Texas. You can find it in the clear streams of the Texas Hill Country. These streams are very clean and cool.

The Guadalupe bass is not just any fish. It is found only in Texas. This makes it unique in the world. People who love fishing often visit Texas to see or catch this fish.

The Guadalupe bass likes to swim in streams with clear water. It often hides among rocks and plants. This helps it stay safe from predators. The fish has a greenish color with dark spots. This color helps it blend into the stream's bottom.

Many people in Texas work to keep the streams clean. They know the importance of the Guadalupe bass. It is important to keep the water clear for the fish to thrive. Kids and adults can help by not littering. They can also help by learning about the fish and sharing what they know.

The Guadalupe bass is more than just a fish. It is a symbol of the natural beauty of Texas. Protecting it helps keep Texas streams healthy and beautiful.

Next time you visit the Hill Country, look for the Guadalupe bass. Remember, it is a unique part of Texas that needs our care.

brief.topic: monarch butterflies fly through Texas every fall on their way to Mexico; they need milkweed plants to lay their eggs; their long trip takes weeks

passageRow.title: Monarch Butterflies' Long Journey
passageRow.body:
## Monarch Butterflies' Long Journey

Every fall, monarch butterflies fly through Texas. They are on their way to Mexico. The journey is long and hard. It takes several weeks to complete.

The monarchs start their trip in Canada. They fly south, passing through many states. Texas is an important stop for them. Here, they find food and rest. They need energy to continue their trip.

Monarch butterflies have bright orange and black wings. These colors make them easy to spot. They fly in groups called "flocks." People in Texas often see them in fields and gardens. The butterflies rest on trees and flowers.

One important plant for monarchs is milkweed. Monarchs need milkweed to survive. This plant is where they lay their eggs. When the eggs hatch, caterpillars eat the milkweed leaves. Eating the leaves makes the caterpillars grow big and strong.

People can help monarchs by planting milkweed. This gives the butterflies a safe place to lay eggs. It also helps caterpillars grow. Planting milkweed can make gardens more lively and colorful.

After their stop in Texas, monarchs continue flying to Mexico. They will spend the winter in the mountains there. In spring, they start their journey back north. The cycle begins again.

Watching monarchs fly through Texas is a special sight. They remind us of nature's wonders. Even small actions, like planting milkweed, can help these amazing butterflies on their journey.

passageRow.topicNotes: Monarch butterflies' migration through Texas and the importance of milkweed.
